ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general broccoli food product entry; no specific Zi Char stir-fried broccoli entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Broccoli — cooked vegetable, stir-fried preparation approximated under cooked brassica category
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Zi Char stir-fried broccoli code. A00LC (broccoli) used as the closest defensible anchor; preparation modifier (stir-fried) noted.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT identifies broccoli as a substance; no specific stir-fried dish code exists. Substance-level code used as the closest defensible anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Zi Char stir-fried broccoli weighs approximately 300 g, typically shared as a side dish among 2–4 diners at a Zi Char restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Broccoli, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (SR Legacy 11091) and HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food Database — Stir-fried vegetables reference
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for plain cooked broccoli from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDCID 170379) were adapted to reflect Zi Char stir-fry preparation: added oil (vegetable oil ~5 g/100g dish), oyster sauce and soy sauce contribution to sodium (~200 mg/100g incremental), and minor cornstarch contribution to carbohydrate. The input record values (energy 83.33 kcal, protein 2.67 g, fat 6.0 g, carbohydrate 5.0 g, sodium 283.33 mg) are consistent with this adaptation and are retained. HPB Singapore database consulted for regional stir-fried vegetable reference ranges to validate plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — Broccoli, cooked
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Broccoli is a non-starchy cruciferous vegetable with a GI of approximately 15 (low) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International GI Tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The stir-fry preparation with small amounts of oyster sauce and cornstarch adds minimal digestible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the GI category. GL per serving calculated as: GI (15) × available carbohydrate per serving (5.0 g/100g × 300 g / 100) = 15 × 15 g / 100 = 2.25, rounded to 2.5 to account for cornstarch contribution.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 283 mg/100g from oyster sauce and soy sauce; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 850 mg sodium, representing ~37% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced oyster sauce and soy sauce or low-sodium alternatives, and avoid additional salt, to keep the per-serving sodium contribution below 500 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~15) and low carbohydrate content (5.0 g/100g); broccoli is a non-starchy vegetable with minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; request minimal cornstarch thickening and reduced oyster sauce to limit added sugar and refined carbohydrate intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.0 g/100g), zero trans fat, and high vitamin C (33.3 mg/100g) and dietary fibre (1.67 g/100g) support cardiovascular health; sodium is the primary concern.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is broadly heart-healthy due to low saturated fat and beneficial phytonutrients from broccoli; sodium reduction through condiment moderation is the key modification for cardiovascular patients.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Broccoli provides sulforaphane and vitamin C (33.3 mg/100g) with anti-inflammatory properties; saturated fat is low at 1.0 g/100g with no trans fat.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce to minimise sodium-driven tissue swelling, and minimal cornstarch to limit refined carbohydrate-driven inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Broccoli (Zi Char Style) is a popular vegetable dish served at Singaporean and Malaysian Zi Char restaurants — casual Chinese-style cook-to-order eateries that specialise in wok-hei dishes. The dish features fresh broccoli florets stir-fried at high heat in a wok with garlic, vegetable oil, oyster sauce, soy sauce, sesame oil, and a light cornstarch slurry that creates a glossy, savoury sauce. The result is tender-crisp broccoli with a rich umami coating and a characteristic smoky wok fragrance. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 83 kcal, 2.7 g protein, 6.0 g fat (predominantly unsaturated from vegetable oil), 5.0 g carbohydrate, 1.7 g dietary fibre, and 283 mg sodium. Vitamin C is well-preserved at 33.3 mg/100g despite cooking. The glycemic index is low (~15), making it suitable for blood glucose management. The primary clinical concern is sodium from condiments; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 850 mg sodium. Saturated fat is low at 1.0 g/100g and trans fat is absent. The dish is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis when condiment quantities are moderated. It is not suitable for shellfish-allergic individuals due to oyster sauce, nor for those requiring gluten-free diets due to soy sauce and oyster sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Zi Char broccoli?
Is broccoli with oyster sauce suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked broccoli data with adjustments for stir-fry preparation (added oil, condiment sodium). The input record values are consistent with this adaptation. No exact Zi Char stir-fried broccoli entry exists in HPB Singapore or USDA databases; adaptation is documented. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database broccoli reference. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish-level approved-source match exists.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDCID 170379 (broccoli, cooked) with stir-fry preparation adjustments; input record values retained as consistent with this adaptation. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this specific Zi Char stir-fried preparation are not available as a composite dish entry in USDA FoodData Central or the HPB Singapore database as of 2026-04-16. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 and cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed as negligible/zero for this vegetable-oil-based stir-fry preparation with no animal fat; consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for cooked broccoli and vegetable oil. 4. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database broccoli reference; stir-fry preparation with minimal cornstarch does not materially alter GI category. 5. Halal status is conditional on oyster sauce brand certification; some oyster sauces may contain non-halal additives — diners should verify. 6. The input record name 'Broccoll' is a typographic variant retained as an alias for retrieval purposes.
